pathophysiology_summary: Allergic conjunctivitis is an IgE-mediated type-I hypersensitivity of the conjunctiva; aeroallergen contact triggers mast-cell degranulation with histamine release causing intense bilateral itch, watery discharge, conjunctival hyperaemia and chemosis, often with concurrent allergic rhinitis. It is benign and vision is preserved. The ground-truth task is to distinguish it from vision-threatening red-eye causes (keratitis, uveitis, acute glaucoma, orbital cellulitis) by confirming preserved vision, absence of severe pain/photophobia/pain-on-eye-movement, and bilaterality; and to exclude anaphylaxis. Itch-predominant, bilateral, watery presentation with allergen exposure is the diagnostic signature; management is topical antihistamine/mast-cell stabiliser, cold compresses, allergen avoidance and no eye-rubbing.

differential.evidence_basis: Bilateral itch-predominant watery eyes with sneezing after outdoor exposure.
Periorbital swelling framing mandates excluding proptosis, painful/limited eye movement, vision change (would be unilateral, painful, febrile).
Also watery/red but grittiness > itch, often preauricular node; itch-predominance argues against.
Purulent, matting discharge — not watery/itchy; argues against.
Explosive facial swelling must prompt screening for airway/systemic features.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Allergic conjunctivitis leads; orbital cellulitis and anaphylaxis must be actively excluded before reassurance.

differential.evidence_basis: Preserved vision, no deep pain/photophobia/pain-on-movement, bilateral, watery, itch-predominant, no airway features — allergic cause confirmed; sight-threatening and systemic emergencies excluded.
Bilateral, painless, normal vision/eye movements — excluded; escalation target if features develop.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After negative red-eye and anaphylaxis screens, allergic conjunctivitis leads; topical antihistamine + avoidance.

differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis; self-limiting with allergen avoidance and topical therapy.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final position is benign allergic conjunctivitis.

red_flags.description: Vision-threatening red eye — reduced vision, severe deep pain, pain on eye movement, photophobia, unilateral, proptosis
red_flags.clinical_significance: Distinguishes benign conjunctivitis from keratitis/uveitis/glaucoma/orbital cellulitis needing urgent ophthalmology.

red_flags.description: Anaphylaxis features — lip/tongue/throat swelling, breathing difficulty

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Interpreting dramatic bilateral lid oedema as orbital cellulitis/anaphylaxis without checking the discriminating features (bilateral, painless, normal vision, no airway features).

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Antibiotic eye drops for allergic eyes
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Prescribing chloramphenicol for watery itchy bilateral eyes — inappropriate; allergic, not bacterial.
Reassuring without confirming preserved vision and absence of severe pain/photophobia.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Formal visual acuity and pupil examination
Assessment of proptosis and eye-movement pain
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Topical antihistamine + cold compress + allergen avoidance; urgent eye care for any red-eye danger sign; 000 for anaphylaxis.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Missing keratitis/uveitis/orbital cellulitis behind dramatic swelling.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Allergic conjunctivitis: topical antihistamine/mast-cell stabiliser, cold compresses, allergen avoidance; oral antihistamine for concurrent rhinitis. Refer red eye with reduced vision, pain, or photophobia.
